Get involved with options.  It has really changed the way I look at investing.  No longer am I satisfied with the meager 8-10% annual gain that they say you should be happy with.  As a whole, my options since May 1st are up over 40%.  The thing is there are some laggards (Costco calls) that I see recovering more after the unfair sell-off from the Whole Foods acquisition and my return will be better.  If Costco hits all time highs, my three June calls are up over 80%.    I own Facebook calls and I think the stock can hit $180 or $190 this year, which will put them up even more.  My Facebook calls are up about 82% right now. I own 7 $JD Jan 2019 $50 strike calls that are up 15%.  If the stock doubles in price in 12 months from current price, my $JD calls will be up over 650%.    I own a Visa call option which is up 60% since I bought it on May 22nd.  In that same time, the stock has "only" gained 13.42%.  I might sell the visa call option and take profits, but not sure.  I'm not saying all this to brag.  I am saying this to show you what is possible if you learn how to invest/trade in stocks that are either growing or unfairly knocked down like Costco was.  Options have really opened up a new world of investing for me and with that, I have made a higher return in 4.5 months, than I could have ever imagined making in 1-2 years.

I'm still a novice when it comes to options so I can only speak from my own experience. By the way, each option is a contract for 100 shares.   Early last week when the stock price was around $13 I bought 100 options:  > 50 NQ 12/21/2013 15.00 C @$2.16  > 50 NQ 12/21/2013 16.00 C @$1.91  Last week was a bit rough; when the stock dropped to $9.50 my options lost half their value. Thankfully the stock rose again and they've since recovered (in fact I'm ever so slightly ahead). So a ~25% drop in stock price caused a ~50% drop in the value of my options.   As I said I'm a novice and these were the first options I've ever bought. I'm still hazy on how you figure out what they SHOULD be worth if the stock rises to a certain point.

If your unsure of what to do and have the feeling things might go down, use the 3:1 rule.  If your buy $500 in stock, then buy ~$167 in puts  -	ATVI $40 put 2/15 have ok volume -	2 contracts @ $0.90 $180  If the stock goes up, you’ll only be out 180 max (not including your stock gains)  If it goes down, you will be hit very minimally on stock value but should make up the difference with your puts.

Theta hurts you if you dont reach your strike but if the tech analysis is lookin correct then that shouldnt be a problem. All of your profit comes down to the true “inherent value” of the option. Last week i bought in a bunch of GDX $19.5 11/23  Puts at close on wednesday (thursday was down for thanksgiving) at $0.04. Pretty dirt cheap and the breakeven on that strike is very close to the written price ($19.46). GDX gapped down as per my TA and finished the day $19.26 and RH autosold my options for $20 a contract. To sum it up, Theta crush will *kill* you doing it this way if you cant reach strike but if TA follows right then you should be fine. As you could imagine it is a lot safer to do ITM puts with this strategy than OTM but to each his own.
